coredata.dc:title: Insights into the antibacterial mechanism of iron doped carbon dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Chao
Duan, Meilin
Shi, Yanfeng
Liu, Hao
Zhang, Pengfei
Zuo, Yuhui
Yan, Lei
Xu, Yuanhong
Niu, Yusheng
coredata.dc:description: Antibacterial nanomaterials provide promising alternative strategies to combat the bacterial infection due to deteriorating resistance. However, few have been practically applied due to the lack of clear antibacterial mechanisms. In this work, we selected good-biocompatibility iron-doped CDs (Fe-CDs) with antibacterial activity as a comprehensive research model to systematically reveal the intrinsic antibacterial mechanism. Through energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) mapping of in situ ultrathin sections of bacteria, we found that a large amount of iron was accumulated inside the bacteria treated with Fe-CDs. Then, combining the data of cell level and transcriptomics, it can be elucidated that Fe-CDs could interact with cell membranes, enter bacterial cells through iron transport and infiltration, increase intracellular iron levels, trigger increased reactive oxygen species (ROS), and lead to disruption of Glutathione (GSH)-dependent antioxidant mechanisms. Excessive ROS further leads to lipid peroxidation and DNA damage in cells, lipid peroxidation destroys the integrity of the cell membrane, and finally leads to the leakage of intracellular substances resulting in bacterial growth inhibition and death. This result provides important insights into the antibacterial mechanism of Fe-CDs and further provides a basis for the deep application of nanomaterials in biomedicine.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Antibacterial mechanism
Antibacterial effect
Transcriptome analysis
Carbon dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Cellular levels studies of the bactericidal mechanism.
Molecular level studies of the bactericidal mechanism.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of Fe-CDs: (A) TEM images and the particle size distribution (inset). (B) AFM and the height analysis (inset). (C) FTIR spectra of Fe-CDs. (D) XRD characterization. (E) XPS spectra of Fe 2p. (F) XPS spectra of O 1 s.

(A) Antibacterial activity of Fe-CDs toward E. coli. (B) Antibacterial activity of ferrous iron and ferric iron. (C) EDTA-2Na inhibits bacterial death induced by Fe-CDs. (D) Representative fluorescence-stained live/dead E. coli cells after different treatments (Scale bar = 25 µm). The error bars represent the standard deviation for three measurements (n = 3).

Mechanistic study for understanding the antibacterial property of Fe-CDs. (A) Cell outer membrane permeability of E. coli under Fe-CDs treatments. (B) Leakage of protein from E. coli after being treated with Fe-CDs. (C) SEM microscopic images of E. coli. (D) TEM photographs of ultrathin sections of bacteria. (E) Fe Element Image of Bacteria. (F) Metabolic activity in E. coli treated by Fe-CDs. (G) ROS level of E. coli under Fe-CDs treatments. (H) Loss of GSH in E. coli treated by Fe-CDs. (I) MDA assay for lipid peroxidation induced by Fe-CDs in E. coli. (J) Genomic DNA degradation in E. coli treated by Fe-CDs. The error bars represent the standard deviation for three measurements (n = 3), Asterisks indicate statistically significant differences from controls (*p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001, ****p < 0.0001).

RNA-Seq gene expression profiles of Fe-CDs treated E. coli compared with control group. (A) Numbers of DEGs. (B) The volcano plot of DEGs. (C) KEGG enrichment analysis of DEGs. (D) Go enrichment analysis of DEGs.

Analysis of DEGs in E. coli. (A) DEGs analysis of membrane transport. (B) Phage shock. (C) Biofilm and cell movement. (D) Oxidative stress. Up-regulated genes were shown in red and down-regulated genes in blue. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Antibacterial mechanism of the Fe-CDs against E. coli.

head.ce:title: Insights into the antibacterial mechanism of iron doped carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Biomedical Engineering, College of Life Science, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Biomedical Engineering, College of Life Science, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Urology Key Laboratory of Urinary System Diseases, The Affiliated Hospital of Qingdao University, Qingdao 266003, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Urology Key Laboratory of Urinary System Diseases
The Affiliated Hospital of Qingdao University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Urology Key Laboratory of Urinary System Diseases, The Affiliated Hospital of Qingdao University, Qingdao 266003, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Chao Huang and Meilin Duan contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Antibacterial nanomaterials provide promising alternative strategies to combat the bacterial infection due to deteriorating resistance. However, few have been practically applied due to the lack of clear antibacterial mechanisms. In this work, we selected good-biocompatibility iron-doped CDs (Fe-CDs) with antibacterial activity as a comprehensive research model to systematically reveal the intrinsic antibacterial mechanism. Through energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) mapping of in situ ultrathin sections of bacteria, we found that a large amount of iron was accumulated inside the bacteria treated with Fe-CDs. Then, combining the data of cell level and transcriptomics, it can be elucidated that Fe-CDs could interact with cell membranes, enter bacterial cells through iron transport and infiltration, increase intracellular iron levels, trigger increased reactive oxygen species (ROS), and lead to disruption of Glutathione (GSH)-dependent antioxidant mechanisms. Excessive ROS further leads to lipid peroxidation and DNA damage in cells, lipid peroxidation destroys the integrity of the cell membrane, and finally leads to the leakage of intracellular substances resulting in bacterial growth inhibition and death. This result provides important insights into the antibacterial mechanism of Fe-CDs and further provides a basis for the deep application of nanomaterials in biomedicine.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The increasing incidence of bacterial infections has become a global concern and threatens human life and health [1]. More worrisome was that the abuse of antibiotics has led to increasing bacterial resistance while traditional antibiotics have become less effective against bacteria [2]. Therefore, creating novel and effective antimicrobials becomes crucial [3]. Recently, nanomaterials including carbon-based nanomaterials, metal-based nanomaterials, and metal–organic frameworks have shown promising results in treating bacterial infections [4]. Among them, carbon quantum dots have received extensive attention as new alternatives to traditional antimicrobial agents due to their simple synthesis, microbial adhesion, low toxicity, and good biocompatibility characteristics [5,6]. In addition, in order to give CDs a richer function and enhance their efficiency, researchers widely adopt the strategy of metal doping to achieve this purpose, and diversified metal-doped CDs have become a landmark application research model [7,8].

Due to the abundance of iron resources on earth and the inherent high biocompatibility of iron, various forms of iron were used in biomedicine and other fields [9,10]. Iron oxide nanoparticles have been approved by the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) for clinical use in the treatment of iron deficiency anemia [11]. It has also been reported that nano-decocted ferrous sulfide can lead to ferroptosis in bacteria to combat a series of conditions caused by pathogen invasion [12]. CDs have a simple structure and small volume, which make ultra-small CDs have stronger permeability and enhance their biological application capabilities [13,14]. The excellent quantum characteristics of carbon dots have a wide range of research and applications in industry, while the quantum properties of CDs give more therapeutic access to biomedicine. The excellent quantum characteristics of carbon dots have a wide range of research and applications in industry, while the quantum properties of CDs give more therapeutic access to biomedicine [15–22]. Importantly, the loading of metal elements on CDs helps functionalized metal species smoothly enter the cellular system through CDs, triggering a series of significant stress pathways. Therefore, iron-doped CDs combine the catalytic properties of metals and the optical properties and size effects of quantum dots (QDs), and then efficiently fight bacteria through synergistic effects with chemical kinetics, photothermal, and photodynamic pathways, which was a powerful model for a nano-antimicrobial platform but mostly rely on substrate catalysis and external energy assistance [23]. The reaction mechanism of metal-doped CDs themselves was still a black-box, which greatly limits the deep application of metal-doped CDs in biomedical fields.

In this study, ultrasmall iron-doped CDs were prepared to systematically their intrinsic antibacterial performance and reaction mechanism. Taking Escherichia coli (E. coli) as a research model, the effect of Fe-CDs on bacterial activity was illustrated by detecting the effects of Fe-CDs on the bacterial cell membrane and bacterial metabolic activity. As illustrated in Scheme 1 , the underlying molecular mechanisms of Fe-CDs on Escherichia coli were also systematically elucidated by transcriptomics studies. By combining biochemical methods with transcriptomic results, a more comprehensive and systematic understanding of the potential antibacterial mechanism of Fe-CDs has been obtained, which was conducive to further advancing the application of nanomaterials in biomedicine.

All reagents were obtained from commercial suppliers and used without further purification. Ultrapure water with a specific resistance of 18.2 MΩ was used throughout the experiment. The microorganisms were obtained from Qingdao Hope Bio-Technology Co., ltd, Qingdao, China.

Fe-CDs were synthesized by a slight modification of the previously reported synthesis method of Carbon Nanodots [24]. Briefly, 1 g of citric acid, 1 g of FeCl3·H2O, and 2 g of urea were dissolved in 10 mL of ultrapure water, then heated for 1 min and 45 s in a microwave oven (Galanz G90F25CN3LN-C2(T1), China), and the product was obtained by dialysis drying.

The morphology of Fe-CDs was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, HITACHI HT7700, Japan) and atomic force microscopy (AFM, Bruker Dimension Icon, USA). The surface functional groups were obtained over the wavelength region from 4000 to 500 cm−1 using a Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS500 apparatus. Crystal structure analysis of Fe-CDs by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Rigaku Smart Lab, Japan). Elemental analysis was performed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific ESCALAB Xi+, USA) and inductively coupled plasma emission spectrometer (ICP-OES, Agilent 5110, USA).

Membrane integrity of treated and untreated bacteria was investigated by morphological observation, outer membrane permeability, LIVE/DEAD bacterial staining, and protein leakage assay. The morphology and internal changes of bacteria were observed by scanning electron microscope (SEM, ZEISS Sigma500, Germany) and transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL JEM-2100, Japan). The permeability of bacterial cell membranes was determined using the N-Phenyl-1-naphthylamine (NPN) method. The membrane integrity of bacterial cells was monitored on a fluorescence microscope (Nikon ECLIPSE Ni-U Japan) using SYTO 9 and propidium iodide (PI) fluorescence imaging. BCA protein quantification kit (Vazyme Biotech Co., China) was used to measure the concentration of leaked protein. Oxidative stress was assessed by measuring intracellular ROS production and intracellular GSH activity by measuring bacterial cells with 2, 7-dichlorofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) and GSH and GSSG Assay Kit (Beyotime Biotech. Inc.), respectively. The experimental procedure was detailed in Supplementary Material.

Total RNA from E. coli was extracted using an RNA extraction kit, according to the manufacturer’s protocol. Following completion of RNA purity and concentration and integrity assessments. Six sequencing libraries were constructed and divided into two groups, with three biological replicates in each group, and sequenced using the BGISEQ platform. All differential expression analyses were performed with DESeq2 (version 1.24.0).

All of the experimental results were expressed as the mean ± standard deviation (n = 3). The statistical analysis was performed with GraphPad Prism 8 and Origin 2022 software. The data were expressed with one-way ANOVA procedure followed by Duncan’s tests at P < 0.05.

To investigate the antibacterial mechanism of metal-doped CDS, a nano-research platform of Fe-CDs was constructed by microwave method and systematically characterized it. The stability of nanomaterial is an important index to investigate the stability of its application. Therefore, we observed the aqueous solution of Fe-CDs for a long time and found that no obvious agglomeration and sedimentation occurred in the 6-day period, and its dispersion stability was good (Fig. S1). The morphology and size of Fe-CDs were observed from TEM and AFM images. According to the TEM images (Fig. 1 A), the Fe-CDs were spherical and well dispersed, and the average particle diameter obtained from the inset of the TEM particle diameter statistics map (inset of Fig. 1A) was 2.88 ± 0.72 nm. High-resolution morphology of Fe-CDs was observed (Fig. S2). The AFM image (Fig. 1B and Fig. S3) and height analysis (inset of Fig. 1B) confirmed the good mono-dispersity of the nanodots, with an average height of about 3 nm, indicating that the number of layers of the prepared product was relatively small. The surface functional groups of the as-prepared Fe-CDs were further confirmed by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy. Spectra were obtained in the 4000 to 500 cm−1 wavelength using Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS500. As depicted in Fig. 1C, Fe-CDs owned a carboxyl characteristic wide peak at 3500 ∼ 3200 cm−1. The peak appeared at ∼ 3300 cm−1 for Fe-CDs, which may represent the amino stretching vibration. The peak at 1630 ∼ 1500 cm−1 should be the amide band or C=O stretching vibration [25]. The peak at 1382 ∼ 1310 cm−1 represented a C-N stretching vibration. The peak at ∼ 1000 cm−1 may be due to the stretching vibration of Fe–O [26]. ICP-OES was also applied to quantify the amount of the metal element in our products. The ICP-OES result shows that the element proportion of the products was 9.4528 % Fe. The crystal structure of Fe-CDs (Fig. 1D) was further confirmed by X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis. The main peak was 2θ = 25.689°, corresponding to C (ICSD617290). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was very sensitive to the elements on the surface of the sample, which can be used to identify the types of elements and the corresponding chemical states (Fig. S4). Fe 2p XPS spectrum was plotted in Fig. 1E, the peak at 709.5 eV belong to 2p3/2 orbital electrons of Fe2+, the two component peaks (711.8 eV and 724.7 eV) were attributed to the Fe3+ [27]. Fe 2p region has significantly split spin–orbit components (Δ metal = 13.1 eV). Due to the low content of Fe2+, the Fe2+ component in the Fe 2p1/2 orbital was not fitted, but it conforms to the binding energy difference between the Fe 2p orbital. The peat at 715.8 eV was attributed to the overlap of the shake-up satellite of oxidized iron. Furthermore, as shown in the O 1 s core level XPS spectra (Fig. 1F), the peak at 531.4 eV was formed by carboxylate oxygen[28]. The apparent peak at 532.7 eV should be attributed to adsorbed oxygen [29]. The optical property of Fe-CDs of Photoluminescence Spectroscopy (PL) spectra and photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) in Fig. S5, Fig. S6, and Table S1.

We tested the antibacterial properties of Fe-CDs by growing plankton-bearing bacteria and Fe-CDs in water, followed by counting colony-forming units (CFU) on Agar plates. As shown in Fig. 2 A, we can notice that as the concentration of Fe-CDs increases, the bactericidal efficiency also increases, and Fe-CDs (400 μg mL−1) reduces the viability of E. coli by ＞ 5 log CFU/mL within 30 min. The bactericidal effect of Fe-CDs on E. coli was more significant than on Staphylococcus aureus (Fig. S7). This experimental result was similar to another previous study [30]. This was attributed to the specific cell structure of Gram-positive bacteria, which has a thicker cell wall structure compared to Gram-negative bacteria.

To further investigate the antibacterial active ingredients in Fe-CDs, we incubated EDTA-2Na (EDTA-2Na) with bacteria and added them to Fe-CDs. The results showed that EDTA-2Na could significantly eliminate the antibacterial activity of Fe-CDs (Fig. 2C and Fig. S7), and iron played a crucial role in Fe-CDs. Next, we investigated the effects of ferrous (Fe2+) and ferric (Fe3+). As shown in Fig. 2B. The bactericidal effect of CDs with different concentrations on E. coli (Fig. S8). CDs undoped with iron have poor antibacterial ability, Fe2+ has a significant killing effect on E. coli more than Fe3+ but lower than that of Fe-CDs. Shen et al. found similar results [12]. This proves that the antibacterial activity still depends on the overall Fe-CDs. In this study, the antibacterial mechanism was further investigated. In addition, as shown in Fig. S9, the antibacterial properties of freshly prepared Fe-CDs and long-standing Fe-CDs were similar, which indicated their stable antibacterial properties.

For the further development and application of Fe-CDs and carbon-based materials, the research on their antibacterial mechanism has become urgent. In this case, we systematically studied its antibacterial mechanism at the cellular and molecular levels.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular levels studies of the bactericidal mechanism.
ce:section.ce:para: The bacterial cell wall was an important part of bacteria, which plays a vital function in the protection of bacteria. The main reason why bacteria die or become damaged was usually because the contents of the cytoplasm leak out due to damage of the bacterial cell wall. Meanwhile, we also evaluated the membrane integrity of bacterial cell membranes after treatment with Fe-CDs by fluorescent staining of live/dead bacteria. Bacteria were stained with PI and SYTO 9. SYTO 9 typically labels all bacteria in a population, whether or not they survive. However, PI can only penetrate bacteria with damaged cell membranes and emit red fluorescence, which can be used to mark dead cells [31]. As shown in Fig. 2D, the bacterial cells in the blank treatment group showed obvious green fluorescence, and no cells were stained with red fluorescence by PI, indicating that most of the bacteria were in an active state and fewer died bacteria. However, although the Fe-CDs treated bacteria also showed green fluorescence after SYTO 9 staining, almost all of them showed red fluorescence after PI staining. The results showed the serious damage of E. coli cell membrane after Fe-CDs treatment. Slavin et al. found electrostatic attraction between metal nanoparticles and cell membranes and that metal nanoparticles or ions can cause surface irregularities and instability of the cell wall [32]. We measured bacterial outer membrane penetration using the hydrophobic fluorescent probe NPN, which was a probe that has weak fluorescence in aqueous solution and intense fluorescence intensity in hydrophobic environment. Intact Gram-negative bacteria prevent NPN entry, but when the outer membrane was damaged, NPN interacts with phospholipids in the bacterial envelope and becomes more fluorescent, so NPN can be used to detect damage to the outer membrane of bacteria [33]. The uptake of NPN by E. coli treated with Fe-CDs was shown in Fig. 3 A. The results indicated that Fe-CDs treatment could directly promote outer membrane permeabilization. In this regard, the outer membrane of bacteria treated with Fe-CDs was more damaged compared with the control group.

Another intuitive manifestation of bacterial membrane damage was leakage of intracellular material, such as some important proteins. The BCA method was used to detect the protein leakage concentration of bacteria after Fe-CDs treatment. The protein reduces Cu2+ to Cu+ under alkaline conditions, and Cu+ forms a purple complex with BCA molecules. The absorbance of the complex at 562 nm had a linear relationship with the protein concentration within a certain range. It was found that the concentration of proteins leaked by bacteria increased with the concentration of Fe-CDs (Fig. 3B), indicating the damage of cytoplasmic membrane with a certain extent.

To visually observe the bacterial damage, SEM and TEM were used to observe the bacterial morphology (Fig. 3C and Fig. 3D). The image of untreated E. coli showed clear rod-shaped bodies with a smooth surface and obvious membrane integrity, while the cell surface of E. coli became rough, wrinkled, and had a large number of depressions upon the Fe-CDs treatment. This shown that the bacterial cell membrane was damaged and the cell was lysed. A similar phenomenon was found using TEM, where cytoplasmic leakage, cell lysis, and cavities were observed for bacteria treated with Fe-CDs. Interestingly, during the analysis of the elements inside the bacteria, we also found that a large amount of iron appeared in E. coli treated with Fe-CDs (Fig. 3E, Fig. S10, and Table S2), which proved that Fe-CDs had entered the inside of the bacteria. The results of SEM and TEM support the previous conclusions about the structural integrity of bacterial cells. These results above suggested that process of bacterium apoptosis. After the contact with Fe-CDs, the structure of E. coli cell membrane was destructed and its permeability increased. Thus, the interior content of bacterial would be leaked, which resulted in the death of E. coli.

Bacteria can lead to abnormal intracellular energy metabolism after treatment with different nanomaterials. The overall metabolic activity of bacteria was evaluated by Alamar Blue (Resazurin) colorimetric assay [34]. Resazurin was nonfluorescent purple-blue in its oxidized state and turns fluorescent pink when reduced, giving rise to absorbance and fluorescence intensity proportional to the number of viable cells [35]. The results showed that the metabolic activity of E. coli treated with Fe-CDs decreased by 20 % at the 100 μg mL−1 (Fig. 3F), and the metabolism was inhibited. Since some toxicity mechanisms were mainly intracellular, the decrease in metabolism can directly demonstrate the toxic effects of Fe-CDs on the interior of cells [36]. It also further demonstrated the cell membrane damage of the cells [35].

In addition, oxidative stress was also one of the ways that nanomaterials cause bacterial death. Most metal nanoparticles achieve a bactericidal effect by oxidative damage to basic cellular components such as proteins and nucleic acids. To investigate the bactericidal mechanism of Fe-CDs, cellular oxidative stress after Fe-CDs treatment was examined by a highly specific fluorescent probe (DCFH-DA) to measure ROS production. As shown in Fig. 3G, ROS levels were significantly upregulated in E. coli treated with Fe-CDs compared with untreated E. coli. At the same time, glutathione (GSH), as an important intracellular molecule in the bacterial antioxidant defense system, can protect cells from toxic compounds and become an indicator of intracellular oxidative stress [37]. Excessive intracellular ROS leads to an imbalance in the pro-oxidation-antioxidant system, resulting in the oxidation of GSH (-SH) to GSH disulfide (-S-S-). The resulting GSH disulfide does not protect cells from oxidative damage and ultimately leads to cell death. Ellman’s method of Yin et al. was modified to detect the concentration of thiol groups in GSH, and then the formation of GSH disulfide (GSSH) was measured by quantitatively detecting the depletion of GSH [38]. As shown in Fig. 3H, the results showed that the internal GSH level of E. coli was reduced by about 20 % after being treated with low concentration of Fe-CDs (100 μg mL−1). At a higher concentration (200 μg mL−1), its even decreased by about 40 %. Many authors have noted that “oxidative stress” occurs when the production of ROS exceeds its catabolism, leading to cell death. The significant contribution of ROS-mediated oxidative stress to the antibacterial effect of Fe-CDs was confirmed by the reduction of intracellular ROS production and GSH concentration.

Fe ion can react inducing Fenton reactions with bacterial metabolic products of H2O2, producing ROS [39,40]. Excessive production of ROS or decreased activity of the antioxidant system can trigger lipid peroxidation to damage the cell membrane and damage the cell. MDA was one of the products produced by lipid oxidation in organisms, so MDA was widely used as an indicator of lipid oxidation. MDA and thiobarbituric acid (TBA) react to generate MDA-TBA Adduct, a red product with maximum absorption peak at 535 nm. According to MDA determination (Fig. 3I), compared with the negative control, Fe-CDs were significantly increased when the concentration was 25 μg mL−1, and MDA content increased by about 2 times when the concentration was increased to 100 μg mL−1. The lipid components of the cell membrane were oxidized by ROS to form lipid peroxidation products such as MDA, which affects the stability and fluidity of the membrane. The destabilization of the membrane and subsequent pore formation results in leakage of cytoplasmic contents into the extracellular region [41]. In conclusion, the result confirmed that the extracellular protein content was significantly increased due to the action of ROS.

ROS generated by nanomaterials may also cause DNA damage [42]. The genomic DNA of E. coli treated with different concentrations of Fe-CDs was analyzed by agarose gel electrophoresis (Fig. 3 J). The DNA bands of the control group were clear and dense (lane 8), indicating the presence of macromolecular DNA, while the E. coli genomic DNA treated with Fe-CDs showed distinct bands (lanes 2 to 7). When Fe-CDs concentration was 100 μg mL−1 (lane 4), obvious DNA damage occurred, and when the concentration was 200 μg mL−1 (lane 3) or higher (lane 2), Fe-CDs showed extensive damage and abundant DNA fragments. These results suggest that Fe-CDs further affect the nucleic acid of bacterial cells while causing membrane damage, breaking macromolecular DNA into small fragments, thereby interfering with biological functions [43]. The destruction of the integrity of the cell membrane allows metal nanomaterials to easily enter the interior of bacterial cells and achieve antibacterial effects by interfering with DNA replication [44]. On the other hand, metal ions can directly intercalate and bind to intracellular DNA. The results of previous experiments in this study showed that the ROS content of E. coli significantly increased after Fe-CDs treatment. Therefore, the DNA damage was caused by both ROS and Fe-CDs.

Based on these findings, it can be concluded that disruption of membrane integrity, disruption of antioxidant defense systems, degradation of nucleic acids, and metabolic inactivation were the primary antimicrobial mechanisms of Fe-CDs at the cellular level, but the research results were still insufficient. It was necessary to use molecular biology research methods to further study the antibacterial mechanism from the perspective of E. coli.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Molecular level studies of the bactericidal mechanism.
ce:section.ce:para: The potential antibacterial mechanism of the Fe-CDs against E. coli was studied by whole transcriptome RNA sequencing. Transcriptomics showed that, Fe-CDs treatment induced significant changes in E. Coli at the mRNA level. Compared to the control group, a total of 425 genes in the Fe-CDs treatment group were differentially expressed (|log2 (fold change) | > 1.5, Q value ≤ 0.05), of which, 357 differentially expressed genes (DEGs) were up-regulated and 68 DEGs were down-regulated (Fig. 4 A and Table S3), the volcano plots were created to visualize the expression of the difference (Fig. 4B).

DEG-expressed genes were then hierarchically clustered and analyzed by the Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes (KEGG) enrichment and Gene Ontology (GO) enrichment (Fig. 4C and Fig. 4D). To further systematically analyze the DEGs functions, KEGG enrichment analysis was performed to categorize DEGs into different pathways based on bioinformatic databases. As shown in Fig. 4C, the results showed that the main KEGG enriched pathways included ABC transport, biofilm formation, and bacterial chemotaxis, indicating that these pathways played an important role in the antibacterial process of Fe-CDs. A total of 892 GO terms were assigned to 425 DEGs in E. coli, including 312 biological process (BP) terms, 284 cellular component (CC) terms, and 296 molecular function (MF) terms (Fig. 5 D). Among these terms, cellular process (GO:0009987) and metabolic process (GO:0006090) were the main distributed terms in BP ontology. In the ontology of MF, the main categories were gathered at terms binding (GO:0000166) and catalytic activity (GO:0016301). Regarding the ontology of CC, the top categories were in cell (GO:0071944) and cell part (GO:0044464).

The ABC protein transport system catalyzes the absorption of essential nutrients or the excretion of toxic substances, thereby acting as a resistance factor for antimicrobial peptides and antibiotics [45,46]. In this study, the expression of genes related to the ABC transport system, including metN, yehW, and yhdX/Y/Z for amino acid transport (GO:0006865), was significantly up-regulated after treatment with Fe-CDs. These results suggest that the ABC transport system was involved in the stress response of E. coli exposed to Fe-CDs. The two-component system serves as a key sensory pathway that enables bacteria to sense, respond to, and adapt to a wide range of environments and stressors [47]. The BasS/BasR system present in E. coli was a zinc-iron-sensing transcriptional regulator in E. coli, in which BasS was a sensor kinase protein, and BasS/BasR mediates bacterial resistance to high Fe3+ concentrations [48,49]. The BasS gene was up- reglulated17.5-fold in E. coli treated with Fe-CDs compared to the control (Fig. 5A). Overall, these upregulation DEGs indicated that the membrane transport system was significantly affected by the Fe-CDs.

The main way for bacteria acquiring iron was through the use of high-affinity chelators, often referred to as siderophore-dependent iron acquisition. In the subsequent steps of iron utilization, the acquired metals were transferred through intracellular transport pathways, which may include different storage compartments, in order to be directed to the cofactor assembly system and eventual protein targeting. We found that some genes in the ABC transporter were related to iron ion homeostasis (GO:0055072), including fes, fepG/D, and fhuB in iron assimilation by chelation and transport (GO:0033214). Overall, the upregulation of iron uptake genes means the active utilization of iron by cells, which provides opportunities for Fe-CDs, which may become a way for Fe-CDs to enter the interior of bacteria.

Previous studies showed that the antimicrobial activity of Fe-CDs was closely associated with their membrane interactive properties. The membrane was essential for all cell viability. To maintain the integrity of the inner membrane under heat stress, osmotic stress, organic solvent or phage infection, many bacteria activate the phage shock protein (Psp) system to maintain the integrity of the inner membrane in response to heat stress, osmotic stress, organic solvent or phage infection [50]. The E. coli Psp system consists of seven genes: the pspABCDE operon, the regulator pspF, and the effector pspG. We found that compared with the control group, the expression of phage shock protein-related genes was up-regulated, among which the pspG gene in the Psp system was up-regulated by 7.84 times, and the pspA, pspB, and pspD genes were up-regulated by 5.31 times, 7.01 times and 1.73 times respectively ((Fig. 5B). Psp responses were triggered during the dissipation of bacterial proton motive force and secretagogue misplacement, which also indicates defects in the structure and integrity of bacterial cytoplasmic membranes.

In bacteria, Fe2+ and Fe3+ can increase the content of total reactive oxygen species in biofilms and stimulate their biofilm formation through oxidative stress. E. coli can form biofilms on biotic or abiotic surfaces, enhancing its survival in adverse environments. Type II protein secretion systems (T2SS) were molecular machines that facilitate the specific transport of folded periplasmic proteins in Gram-negative bacteria through specialized channels in the outer membrane [51]. Secreted substrates, released into the environment or displayed on the cell surface, help bacteria adapt to complex environments. The main function of T2SS was nutrient acquisition, in addition, T2SS also promotes the secretion of toxins, adhesins, mucus proteins or cytochromes and plays a role in respiration, motility or biofilm formation. The gspI, gspH, gspL genes related to T2SS were up-regulated (Fig. 5C). Simultaneously, the minorsigma transcription factor FliA (RpoF, sigma-28), which was responsible for initiating transcriptionat many genes involved in locomotion, and the wcaj gene related to capsular polysaccharide synthesis were also up-regulated (Fig. 5C). This revealed an adaptive survival strategy undertaken by the E. coli biofilm part in the presence of Fe-CDs treatment.

At the same time, the cell movement of bacteria depends on flagella assembly and chemotaxis, which was also one of the survival strategies of bacteria to relieve environmental stress. Through GO enrichment analysis, it was found that the “flagellar assembly” and “bacterial chemotaxis” pathways were significantly enriched in E. coli, and many flagellar assembly-related genes were overexpressed. The gene flgF was involved in the formation of the flagellar rod, the main component of the flagellar body, which spans the bacterial periplasm. The gene flgA was involved in the formation of the P loop in the loop system (L loop, P loop, and MS loop, respectively) embedded in the cell envelope. In addition, two flagellar motor genes, motA, and motB were upregulated by 5.31 and 3.01 times in E. coli (Fig. 5C), respectively. In conclusion, bacterial chemotaxis can be affected by Fe-CDs.

In this study, the intracellular ROS content of E. coli showed an increasing trend after Fe-CDs treatment (Fig. 3G). To further understand the response of bacterial cells to oxidative stress, the expression levels of genes related to the antioxidant system were examined (Fig. 5D). The alkyl hydroperoxide reductase gene ahpC, the lipid hydroperoxidase gene tpx and the peroxidase genes osmC and bcp were significantly down-regulated. As a result, the higher ROS produced by Fe-CDs overwhelmed the cellular defenses, inhibiting the antioxidant system.

Energy metabolism plays a crucial role in living organisms and was necessary for life activities. In this study, the metabolism of E. coli cells decreased after Fe-CDs treatment, and we detected the expression levels of oxidative phosphorylation-related genes for analysis. Among them, the genes sdhA/C and nuoE related to the tricarboxylic acid cycle (GO: 0006099) and the electron transport chain (GO: 0022900) were down-regulated, and the genes ppk and atpC/G related to the ATP biosynthesis process (GO: 0006754) were also down-regulated (Fig. 5D). Taken together, Fe-CDs can inhibit bacterial growth by disrupting energy metabolism.

In summary, E. coli was used as a model to systematically study the antibacterial mechanism of metal-doped carbon dots. First, Fe-CDs enter the interior of bacteria by osmosis and transport, and then induce bacterial death, such as the increase of ROS, the collapse of GSH dependent antioxidant mechanism, and lipid peroxidation. The transcriptome detected 425 significantly differentially expressed genes at the molecular level, which were enriched in passway of membrane transport, cell and cell membrane permeability, cell motility, oxidative stress, and energy metabolism. In this work, the interaction of Fe-CDs with E. coli was clearly illustrated, providing new insights into the role of metal-doped materials in biology, and it further provides support for the in-depth application of nanomaterials.

Chao Huang: Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Visualization. Meilin Duan: Visualization, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Yanfeng Shi: Methodology, Data curation. Hao Liu: Data curation. Pengfei Zhang: Visualization. Yuhui Zuo: Data curation. Lei Yan: Formal analysis. Yuanhong Xu: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Yusheng Niu: Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21874079), Natural Science Foundation for Distinguished Young Scholars of Shandong Province (ZR2021JQ04), Taishan Scholars Program of Shandong Province (No. Tsqn 201909088), Outstanding Youth Innovation Team of Universities in Shandong Province (2019KJA027).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2023.04.149.

ce:section.ce:para:
